coredata.dc:title: Designed optimization of photoluminescence emission for carbon dots with bright blue emission at 416 nm and mono-exponential decay lifetime
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Abbas Ruwaih, Mohammed
Soheyli, Ehsan
Naji, Jalil
Mutlugun, Evren
Kikhavani, Tavan
Sahraei, Reza
coredata.dc:description: The presented study introduces optimized blue-emissive carbon dots (CDs) with high photoluminescence efficiency up to 65 % at 416 nm, large Stokes shift (69 nm), and full-width at half maximum (FWHM) of 73 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy confirmed the formation of carbon-based bonds as the main component of CDs, with reliable amounts of O, S, and N as dopant components. These features, along with single-exponential time-decay profile at long average lifetime of 10.05 ns, supported the significant role of uniformly distributed mid-gap energy levels in the recombination process. The simplicity, low-cost, non-toxicity, and short reaction time of CDs, along with their excellent emission properties in the deep-blue region, make them suitable for use in environmental monitoring and high-contrast bioimaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Deep-blue emission
High quantum efficiency
Uniform energy levels

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The (A) XRD pattern of CD14 and (B) FTIR results of typical CDs.
XPS pattern of the CDs, and magnified XPS plots with proper peak-fitting for each constituent element.

(A) Detail of materials used for the preparation of CDs (stars show the similar batches). (B) UV–Vis, PLE (λ emis = 420), PL (λ exc = 350) spectra of the CD1-to-CD17 (The digital images of CD14 in solution and powder forms are also included as inset), and (C) PL decay curve of CDs14.

head.ce:title: Designed optimization of photoluminescence emission for carbon dots with bright blue emission at 416 nm and mono-exponential decay lifetime

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ilam University, Ilam 69315-516, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ilam University, Ilam 69315-516, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The presented study introduces optimized blue-emissive carbon dots (CDs) with high photoluminescence efficiency up to 65 % at 416 nm, large Stokes shift (69 nm), and full-width at half maximum (FWHM) of 73 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy confirmed the formation of carbon-based bonds as the main component of CDs, with reliable amounts of O, S, and N as dopant components. These features, along with single-exponential time-decay profile at long average lifetime of 10.05 ns, supported the significant role of uniformly distributed mid-gap energy levels in the recombination process. The simplicity, low-cost, non-toxicity, and short reaction time of CDs, along with their excellent emission properties in the deep-blue region, make them suitable for use in environmental monitoring and high-contrast bioimaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: There is an academic competition to discover, represent, and improve new luminescent materials with desired physicochemical characteristics for commercialization. From these perspectives, carbon dots (CDs) are nice candidates that use facile reaction methods, and inexpensive precursors/reaction tool, to show distinctive properties with wide range of applications in sensing [1], bioimaging [2], light-emitting diodes [3], etc. They also exhibited a non-toxic feature as undeniable prerequisite for the most recent stage in the development of a product for commercialization. CDs are generally highly luminescent when dispersed in a proper solvent. But the recognized limitation that exist in front of CDs is aggregation-caused luminescence quenching when they are precipitated into solid-state powders [4]. This is more difficult for emission in the deep-blue region, at which strong π-π* conjugations exist. Besides, as luminescent CDs deal with electronic energy levels, the experimental parameters are determinative in formation of high-quality samples with proper surface properties.

In this study, aqueous-soluble CDs are prepared via a quick microwave heating approach. Tri-sodium citrate, tartaric acid, and L-cysteine were used at different amounts to optimize the PL emission properties, approaching bright blue emission at 416 nm with PLQY of about 65 %. Considering the simplicity of the preparation method, non-toxicity, surface-rich functionality, and bright luminosity, the presented CDs are excellent nominates for sensing and nano-medicine.

Materials were used as received, including tartaric acid (TA, C4H6O6, Merck, >99.5 %), tri-sodium citrate dihydrate (TSC, Na3C6H5O7·2H2O, Merck, 99.99 %), L-cysteine (L-cys, C3H7NO2S, Merck, >97 %), and double-distilled water.

To investigate the effect of different experimental variables on the properties of the CDs, amounts of precursors were optimized. The amounts of chemicals (variable parameters) used for synthesizing CDs are presented as inset in Fig. 3A. Typically, a required amount of the L-cys was weighted and then dissolved in 15 mL of double-distilled water using a stirring magnet bar under a mild heating condition. Then, TSC was added to the solution and dissolved completely (Solution A). In the meantime, 0.48 g of TA was dissolved in 12 mL of double-distilled water to prepare TA precursor (Solution B). After that, a specific volume of the solution B was added to the solution A and stirred vigorously to make it homogeneous. Finally, the mixture was transferred to a domestic microwave subjecting to microwave heating at constant irradiation power of 600 W for 10 min. After that, 0.1 g of white powder of CDs was dissolved thoroughly in 5 mL of double-distilled water and used for further optical measurements.

Structural order of the produced CDs was investigated using X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis (Fig. 1 A). Resulting XRD pattern showed broad, low-intensity peaks at 2θ = 24° (0 0 2) and 45° (1 0 0), indicating the presence of weakly crystalline carbonized-region in the core and an amorphous-like shell [5].

Fig. 1B represents FT-IR spectra for two samples at different concentrations of TSC. The spectra demonstrated the presence of carboxylic and carbonyl groups at 1400 cm−1 and 1700 cm−1, which are characteristic features of carbon-based nanoscale materials. The broad O–H band indicates the presence of water in the samples. The absence of thiol-related S-H band of the L-cys, which is typically found at around 2500 cm−1, also showed that these groups have been fully decomposed, leading to contribution of sulfur species to the main structure of the prepared CDs as confirmed by C-S signal at 1060 cm−1. These results also suggest that there is no significant change in the spectra when the amount of main carbon source (TSC) changes in this study.

Results of full spectrum X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey, along with their corresponding high-resolution plots, are shown in Fig. 2 . The distinctive peaks in the plot confirm the presence of C, O, N, and S as the primary constituent elements of the synthesized CDs. The high-resolution data allows the identification of specific peaks for each element. The C1s profile exhibits three peaks, with the peaks attributed to the CC (sp2 carbons) and C-C (sp3 carbons) bonds in aromatic rings (284.8 eV), O-C-O and C-S bonds (286.6 eV), and HO-CO bonds (288.5 eV). The O1s spectrum also displayed four peaks at 531.3 eV, 532.4 eV, 533.2 eV, and 535.5 eV, corresponding to C-O-C, O-CO, and C-OH bonds, respectively. The N1s spectrum included two peaks at 399.6 and 401.3 eV, corresponding to C-N and N-H bonds, respectively. The S2p spectrum also showed spin–orbit peaks of C-S-C at 163.4 eV and 164.6 eV, associated with S2p3/2 and S2p1/2 levels, respectively. The corresponding elemental analysis indicates that the synthesized CDs contain C, O, N, and S in atomic proportions of 48.28 %, 29.1 %, 2.27 %, and 2.17 %, respectively. These results demonstrated the formation of heteroatoms-doped CDs, which improve emission properties of the synthesized CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–Vis, PLE, PL, and TRPL spectra
ce:section.ce:para: A series of experiments at different amounts of constituents were designed (as tabulated in Fig. 3 A). Here we provide PL, PLE, and UV–Vis data for each sample and then discuss them (Fig. 3B). First, we focus on the general aspects of the plots. As an example, we choose the best emission results (CD14). As shown in the Fig. 3B, the absorption spectrum shows a clear absorption band at 347 nm (has been repeated in most of the other prepared CDs) which can be attributed to the n → π* transitions related to the CO bonds. In accordance with the UV–Vis plot, the PLE peak has been located at 347 nm showing a uniform band structure. However, with a relatively large Stokes shift (of about 69 nm) an intense PL emission signal was located at the deep-blue region of the spectrum around 416 nm. Such a large Stokes shift is an intrinsic feature of CDs confirming the main role of mid-gap states in recombination of charge carriers. Besides, as another evidence, the PL spectrum is too broad (67 nm) which demonstrates the remarkable contribution of energy levels. The bright deep-blue emission of the prepared CDs in both colloidal and powder forms appeared under 365 nm UV lamp (Inset of Fig. 3B-CD14). To further confirm our outcomes regarding the recombination processes, TRPL measurement was performed for CD14 (Fig. 3C). Results showed a single-exponential fit for decay-lifetime plot with an average lifetime of about 10.05 ns. This value is a relatively long lifetime for luminescent CDs [6–8]. It also implies the uniformity of energy levels as a single pathway was obtained for the recombination of excited carriers [9]. The PLQY of the colloidal sample was also calculated using comparative formula [10] to be around 65 % (via 9,10-diphenylanthracene standard dye with PL peaks at 406 and 427 nm).

As summarized in Fig. 3B, various optimizations were considered on materials concentration to reach the best result. The amount of TSC was found to be determinative on the emission intensity of CDs, as employing 0.1 g of TSC leads to a quite low emission intensity and low amount of final reaction product (CD1 and CD6), while it showed an enhanced PL intensity upon increase in the amount of TSC. Besides, L-cys with a well-known thiol group is able to decompose under high temperatures and pressures, introducing sulfur into the CDs composites [10]. Therefore, when a low amount of 0.02 g of L-cys is used, the intensity of the spectrum decreased and a very weak peak was observed (like CD9, CD12, CD16, CD17). However, when its value has been increased (0.08 and 0.14 g), the intensity of PL (and PLE) spectra improved remarkably (like CD2, CD4). It can be attributed to the emergence of new energy levels that are related to the sulfur species in the structure which work as new radiative recombination centers for charge carriers. Finally, and attractively, TA molecules play a role in modifying the structure of prepared CDs along with addition of oxygen to the main lattice. In the absence of TA, no emission spectrum is observed (like CD1, CD5, CD7, and CD17) due to the low density of oxygen-related energy levels, while the presence of a large amount of TA has a negative effect on the intensity of the spectrum (like CD6, CD8). Based on these evaluations, the best PL emission result was obtained for CDs prepared with 0.8 g TSC, 0.08 g L-cys, and 8 mL of TA under 10 min of 600 W microwave power. Also, the current expeditious microwave technique has successfully produced non-toxic and high-quality aqueous-soluble CDs that can be easily scaled up within a brief reaction period.

The preparation of aqueous-soluble luminescent CDs with a dominant emission peak at 416 nm and a PLQY of 65 % was achieved through a cost-effective and straightforward microwave-assisted method using readily available precursors. The optimized sample maintained its deep-blue emission characteristics in powdered form, displaying consistent optical properties. Furthermore, the sample exhibited a prolonged average lifetime of approximately 10 ns, characterized by a single-exponential decay profile. These outstanding optical and structural attributes underscore the potential of these CDs as versatile nanomaterials for a wide array of applications, including optoelectronics, nano-biotechnology, and sensing.

Mohammed Abbas Ruwaih: Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis. Ehsan Soheyli: Methodology, Conceptualization, Validation, Supervision, Writing – original draft. Jalil Naji: Supervision, Project administration. Evren Mutlugun: Validation, Resources. Tavan Kikhavani: Software, Data curation. Reza Sahraei: Writing – review & editing, Resources.
The E.S. and R.S. would like to acknowledge funding support received from the Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) with the Grant Number of 4013040.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
